los_found_in_corpus: LO1: Define three distinct types of AI hallucination — factual, attributional, and faithful-but-wrong — and give a concrete example of each.
LO2: Explain why the token-prediction mechanism makes hallucination a structural property of LLMs, not a bug that can be patched away.
LO3: Describe two contributing causes — training data gaps and RLHF incentives — that make hallucination more likely in practice.
LO4: Identify real-world contexts — legal, medical, academic — where hallucination has caused documented harm.
LO5: List three detection and mitigation strategies (verification, grounding, and retrieval-augmented generation) that reduce hallucination risk without eliminating it.

notes: q2 stem spells out 'LLM (Large Language Model)' inline.
q3 stem spells out 'Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)' inline.
q5 distractor references 'temperature' — a prerequisite term defined in corpus §2 (topic 3.6); fair for this audience.
All questions are answerable by a beginner who read this topic corpus and nothing else.

verdict: Pass — all five checks clean: bias_defense_applied=true, positional max_share=0.40 (at threshold, not over), length longest_correct_rate=0.0, distractor absurd_rate=0.0, all 5 LOs covered with no orphan questions, all stems beginner-fair and on-scope.
